description: Temporary increase in body temperature above 38C (100.4F), usually indicating the body is fighting an infection.
key_questions: What is your current temperature reading?
When did the fever first start?
Is the fever constant or does it come and go?
Do you have any other symptoms like cough, sore throat, body aches, or rash?
Have you traveled recently to any tropical or endemic areas?
How much water and fluids have you been drinking today?
How many cups of coffee, tea, or caffeinated drinks do you have daily?
Do you drink alcohol? If yes, how often?
Have you been in contact with anyone who is sick?
Are you currently taking any medications?
dos: Drink at least 2-3 liters of water throughout the day — dehydration worsens fever
Use Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) to replenish lost electrolytes
Rest completely — your body heals fastest during sleep
Wear lightweight, breathable cotton clothing
Keep the room well-ventilated with fresh air circulation
Use a damp cloth on the forehead and neck for comfort — sponge bathing with lukewarm water
Take paracetamol (acetaminophen) as directed for temperature above 38.5C
Monitor temperature every 4-6 hours and keep a written log
Eat light, easily digestible foods: rice porridge, bananas, toast, clear soups, khichdi
Drink warm turmeric milk (haldi doodh) — natural anti-inflammatory
Sip ginger tea with honey — helps with body aches and immunity
Take a lukewarm (not cold) bath if temperature exceeds 39C
donts: Do NOT drink coffee or caffeinated beverages — caffeine is a diuretic that worsens dehydration
Do NOT consume alcohol — it dehydrates and suppresses your immune system
Do NOT take cold/ice baths — shivering actually raises core body temperature
Do NOT bundle up in heavy blankets — this traps heat and prevents cooling
Do NOT skip meals — your body needs energy to fight the infection
Do NOT exercise or do any strenuous physical activity
Do NOT self-medicate with antibiotics — antibiotics do not work on viral infections
Do NOT ignore fever above 39.4C (103F) lasting more than 2 days
Do NOT give aspirin to children or teenagers — risk of Reye's syndrome
Do NOT eat spicy, oily, or heavy foods — they are harder to digest when sick
home_remedies: Tulsi (Holy Basil) tea: Boil 8-10 tulsi leaves in water, add honey — natural antipyretic
Ginger-honey drink: Grate fresh ginger in hot water with a spoon of honey
Turmeric milk: Warm milk with 1/2 tsp turmeric powder — anti-inflammatory
Coriander seed water: Boil 1 tbsp coriander seeds in water, strain, drink warm
Rice starch water (kanji): Boil rice with extra water, strain, drink the starchy liquid — rehydrating
Cold compress: Damp cloth on forehead and armpits to naturally lower temperature
dietary_guidelines: Eat: clear broths, soups, rice porridge, bananas, boiled potatoes, toast, yogurt, coconut water
Drink: water, ORS, herbal teas (ginger, chamomile, peppermint), warm lemon water
Avoid: fried foods, spicy foods, dairy (except yogurt), caffeine, alcohol, sugary drinks, processed foods
warning_signs: Fever lasting more than 3 days without improvement
Severe headache with stiff neck — possible meningitis
Persistent vomiting or inability to keep fluids down
Dark or reduced urine output — severe dehydration sign

description: Viral infection of the upper respiratory tract causing nasal congestion, runny nose, sneezing, and mild sore throat. Usually self-limiting within 7-10 days.
key_questions: When did your symptoms first start?
Do you have a runny nose, stuffy nose, or both?
Is your nasal discharge clear, yellow, or green?
Do you have a sore throat along with the cold?
Have you had a fever? If so, how high?
How many hours of sleep are you getting each night?
Do you take any Vitamin C supplements or eat citrus fruits regularly?
Are you exposed to air conditioning or indoor heating for long periods?
Do you smoke or are you around secondhand smoke?
Has anyone at home or work been sick recently?
dos: Rest as much as possible — aim for 8-10 hours of sleep during illness
Drink warm fluids frequently: herbal tea, warm water with honey and lemon, clear broths
Gargle with warm salt water (1/2 teaspoon salt in a cup of warm water) 3-4 times daily
Use saline nasal drops or spray to relieve congestion naturally
Keep indoor air humid using a humidifier or by placing a bowl of warm water in the room
Wash hands frequently with soap for 20 seconds to prevent spreading
Take steam inhalation — add a few drops of eucalyptus oil for better effect
Eat foods rich in Vitamin C: oranges, guava, amla, kiwi, bell peppers, broccoli
Take zinc lozenges within 24 hours of symptoms — shown to reduce cold duration
Eat chicken soup — scientifically shown to reduce inflammation and thin mucus
Drink kadha (Ayurvedic immunity drink): ginger, tulsi, black pepper, turmeric boiled in water
donts: Do NOT take antibiotics — colds are viral, antibiotics only work on bacteria
Do NOT smoke or vape — it directly irritates the respiratory tract and delays healing
Do NOT drink very cold beverages — they worsen throat irritation
Do NOT overuse nasal decongestant sprays beyond 3 days — causes rebound congestion
Do NOT go to work/school during the first 2-3 days — you are most contagious then
Do NOT skip sleep — inadequate rest significantly prolongs recovery
Do NOT drink alcohol — it dehydrates and suppresses immune function
Do NOT exercise intensely — light movement is OK, but heavy workouts delay recovery
Do NOT suppress sneezing — it can damage eardrums and blood vessels
Do NOT touch your face with unwashed hands — this spreads the virus
home_remedies: Honey-lemon-ginger tea: Mix 1 tbsp honey, juice of half a lemon, and grated ginger in hot water
Steam inhalation: Lean over a bowl of hot water with towel over head, breathe deeply for 10 minutes
Tulsi-ginger kadha: Boil tulsi leaves, ginger, black pepper, and cinnamon in water for 15 min
Turmeric gargle: Add 1/2 tsp turmeric and salt to warm water, gargle 3-4 times daily
Honey (raw): Take 1-2 teaspoons before bed — WHO-recommended cough suppressant
Ajwain (carom seed) steam: Add ajwain to boiling water and inhale the steam
dietary_guidelines: Eat: citrus fruits, garlic, ginger, chicken soup, yogurt, warm oatmeal, honey, green leafy vegetables
Drink: warm water, herbal teas, fresh orange juice, warm lemon water, bone broth
Avoid: dairy (increases mucus for some), sugary foods, fried foods, cold drinks, processed foods, alcohol
warning_signs: Symptoms lasting more than 10 days without improvement
Symptoms that worsen after initial improvement
Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath

description: Headaches range from mild tension-type to severe migraines. Tension headaches feel like band pressure. Migraines are throbbing, usually one-sided, with nausea and light sensitivity.
key_questions: Where exactly is the pain? One side, both sides, behind the eyes, or forehead?
Is the pain throbbing/pulsating, or a constant pressure like a tight band?
On a scale of 1-10, how severe is the pain right now?
When did this headache start? Hours ago, yesterday, or has it been weeks?
Are you sensitive to light or sound right now?
Do you feel nauseous or have you vomited?
How many hours of screen time (phone + computer) do you have daily?
How much water have you drunk today?
How many cups of coffee/tea do you drink daily? Did you skip it today?
How many hours of sleep did you get last night?
Are you currently stressed about anything — work, exams, relationships?
Have you eaten today? When was your last meal?
Do you wear glasses? When was your last eye checkup?
Have you taken any painkillers? How often do you take them?
dos: Drink 2-3 glasses of water immediately — dehydration headaches resolve within 30-60 minutes
Rest in a quiet, dark room — especially critical for migraines
Apply a cold compress to the forehead and temples for 15 minutes
Apply a warm compress to the back of the neck — relaxes tension
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen at the FIRST sign of pain — don't wait until it's severe
Follow the 20-20-20 rule: every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds
Maintain a strict sleep schedule — go to bed and wake at the same time daily
Practice deep breathing: 4 seconds in, 7 seconds hold, 8 seconds out
Gently massage temples and the base of the skull in circular motions
Keep a headache diary to identify your personal triggers
Eat regular meals — never skip breakfast
Take regular breaks from screens — at least 10 minutes every hour
Apply peppermint oil to temples — clinically shown to relieve tension headaches
donts: Do NOT take painkillers more than 15 days per month — causes medication overuse headache
Do NOT consume more than 3 cups of coffee daily — and never suddenly stop caffeine
Do NOT stare at screens for hours without breaks — use blue light filter and night mode
Do NOT read or use your phone in a dark room — the contrast strains your eyes severely
Do NOT skip meals — low blood sugar is a major headache trigger
Do NOT drink alcohol when you already have a headache — alcohol dilates blood vessels
Do NOT sleep with poor neck support — invest in a proper pillow
Do NOT ignore a sudden, severe 'thunderclap' headache — seek emergency care immediately
Do NOT use screens 1 hour before bed — blue light disrupts melatonin production
Do NOT clench your jaw — a common unconscious tension trigger
home_remedies: Peppermint oil: Apply diluted peppermint oil to temples — proven tension headache relief
Ginger tea: Boil fresh ginger slices for 10 min — anti-inflammatory, helps migraine nausea
Cold compress: Ice pack wrapped in cloth on forehead for 15 min — numbs the pain
Lavender inhalation: Inhale lavender essential oil or add to a warm bath — calming effect
Clove paste: Crush 2-3 cloves, mix with coconut oil, apply to forehead
Cinnamon paste: Mix cinnamon powder with water, apply to forehead for 30 minutes
dietary_guidelines: Eat: magnesium-rich foods (almonds, spinach, avocado, dark chocolate), omega-3 fatty acids (fish, walnuts), riboflavin (eggs, lean meats, green vegetables)
Drink: at least 8 glasses of water daily, ginger tea, peppermint tea, chamomile tea
Avoid: aged cheese, processed meats, MSG, artificial sweeteners (aspartame), excessive caffeine, red wine, chocolate (for migraine sufferers)
warning_signs: Sudden, severe headache unlike any before (thunderclap) — possible hemorrhage
Headache with fever, stiff neck, confusion — possible meningitis
Headache after head injury — possible concussion
Headache with vision changes, speech difficulty, weakness — stroke warning signs
Headache that wakes you from sleep regularly
